rule.name: String Substitution in MyBatis Mapper (${} Syntax)
rule.rule_desc: Detects use of MyBatis ${} string substitution syntax in mapper XML files.
rule.vuln_desc: ${} performs direct string substitution without parameterization, equivalent to string concatenation in raw JDBC. Any user-controlled value passed through ${} is directly injected into the SQL statement, enabling SQL injection.
rule.developer: Use #{} syntax for all user-supplied values. #{} uses prepared statement parameterization. Reserve ${} only for structural SQL elements like column names or table names that cannot be parameterized, and validate them against a strict allowlist.
rule.reviewer: Replace all ${} occurrences with #{} unless there is a documented justification. For any remaining ${} usage, confirm the value comes from a controlled allowlist, not user input. This can be confirmed as an issue when the matched path is reachable and required controls are missing, weak, or inconsistent.

rule.name: OrderBy Clause with Dynamic Substitution
rule.rule_desc: Detects dynamic ORDER BY clauses using MyBatis string substitution.
rule.vuln_desc: ORDER BY clauses cannot use prepared statement parameters (column names cannot be parameterized), so ${} is commonly used. If the column name originates from user input without allowlist validation, this enables SQL injection via ORDER BY manipulation.
rule.developer: Validate column names and sort directions against an explicit allowlist of permitted values before constructing ORDER BY clauses. Use a mapping table to translate user-friendly names to actual column identifiers.
rule.reviewer: Confirm ORDER BY ${} values are validated against a strict allowlist and cannot be directly supplied by end users. This can be confirmed as an issue when the matched path is reachable and required controls are missing, weak, or inconsistent.

rule.name: selectKey or Provider with Dynamic SQL Building
rule.rule_desc: Detects MyBatis Provider annotations that dynamically build SQL queries.
rule.vuln_desc: SQL Provider classes that build SQL via string concatenation using method parameters can introduce SQL injection if inputs are not validated before inclusion in the dynamic query.
rule.developer: Within Provider classes, validate all dynamic SQL fragments (column names, table names) against allowlists. Use MyBatis SQL builder API (new SQL().SELECT().FROM()) rather than raw string concatenation.
rule.reviewer: Review Provider implementations for unsafe string concatenation of user-controlled values into SQL. This can be confirmed as an issue when the matched path is reachable and required controls are missing, weak, or inconsistent.
